title: Torn Dewclaw in Dogs: First Aid and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog bleeding from a torn dewclaw? Learn if it is an emergency, how to provide first aid, and why your vet needs a photo for triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: A torn dewclaw is a painful injury that usually requires veterinary care within 24 hours to prevent infection, though it is only a life-threatening emergency if bleeding is uncontrollable.

What is a torn dewclaw and how does it happen?

A dewclaw is the small, vestigial nail located higher up on your dog's leg. Because these nails do not touch the ground and wear down naturally, they can grow long and easily snag on carpet, brush, or furniture. When a dewclaw tears, it often exposes the 'quick,' which contains sensitive nerves and blood vessels, leading to pain and significant bleeding.

Is a torn dewclaw considered a veterinary emergency?

  • The urgency level for a torn dewclaw is typically Medium.
  • While it is rarely a life-threatening situation, it is very painful and carries a high risk of infection if left untreated.
  • You should schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 hours.
  • Seek urgent care if the bleeding is profuse and cannot be stopped with pressure, or if your dog is showing signs of extreme distress.

Why should I send my veterinarian a photo of the injury?

  • A clear photo allows the medical team to see exactly where the break occurred.
  • A high-quality image shows whether the nail is still attached, dangling, or completely gone.
  • Visual information helps the staff determine how quickly your dog needs to be seen.
  • It allows the clinic to prepare for either a simple trim or a more involved nail bed procedure.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

First aid for a torn dewclaw in dogs involves a brief physical examination, noting any lameness as an indicator of potentially more serious injury. The wound should be assessed for location, hemorrhage, configuration, penetration of a body cavity or synovial structure, and/or involvement of tendons. Initial steps include applying a sterile, water-based lubricant, clipping the hair, sterile preparation of the skin, and lavage of the wound. Wounds over joints, tendon sheaths, or tendons, puncture wounds, and those that expose or penetrate bone should be explored thoroughly for injury to underlying structures. Hemorrhage may need to be controlled before transportation to a veterinary facility.

Chapter: Emergency, Surgery, General Principles

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1707)

faqs.question: How can I stop the bleeding at home?
faqs.answer: Apply firm, steady pressure with a clean cloth for 5 to 10 minutes. If you have styptic powder or cornstarch, you can dip the nail in it to help the blood clot.

faqs.question: Should I try to cut the dangling nail myself?
faqs.answer: It is generally best to wait for a professional. Cutting a damaged nail can be extremely painful and may cause your dog to bite out of reflex. A vet can provide local numbing or sedation if needed.

faqs.question: Will my dog need antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Because the nail bed is close to the bone, vets often prescribe a short course of antibiotics and anti-inflammatory pain medication to prevent infection and manage discomfort.
